---
title: "Speed up with GPUs"
description: "Enable GPU for GPU-capable components today; rapids-singlecell coming soon."
categories: [openpipeline, openpipeline_rapids]
order: 86
---

[`openpipeline`]{.pkg-tag .pkg-openpipeline}<span class="swatch openpipeline"></span>
[`openpipeline_rapids`]{.pkg-tag .pkg-openpipeline_rapids}<span class="swatch openpipeline_rapids"></span>

There are two paths to GPU acceleration: components that wrap **natively
GPU-capable tools** (available today), and GPU drop-ins for the remaining steps
via `openpipeline_rapids` (coming soon).

## The `gpu` label

Several components wrap tools that natively support GPU acceleration, for
example `cellbender`, `scvi`, `scanvi`, `scarches`, and `celltypist`. These
already carry a **`gpu` label** (alongside their usual CPU/memory labels), which
marks them as able to run on a GPU. The `gpu` label works like the other
resource labels, which have their own [Tune compute resources](tune-resources.qmd)
guide.

By default they still run on CPU. To actually schedule them onto a GPU, adjust
the Nextflow config to request GPU resources for the `gpu` label, using the
[`accelerator`](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#accelerator)
directive:

```groovy
// gpu.config
process {
  withLabel: gpu {
    accelerator = 1
    // plus any executor- / runtime-specific GPU settings
  }
}
```

```bash
nextflow run https://packages.viash-hub.com/vsh/openpipeline.git \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/integration/scvi_leiden/main.nf \
  --input cohort_01_multisample.h5mu --output cohort_01_integrated.h5mu \
  --publish_dir results/ \
  -c gpu.config
```

The exact settings depend on your executor (local, AWS Batch, an HPC scheduler)
and container runtime, see the
[Nextflow accelerator / GPU documentation](https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/process.html#accelerator)
for how to configure it on your platform.

## Coming soon: `openpipeline_rapids`

:::{.callout-important}
## Coming soon
`openpipeline_rapids` is not released yet. This section previews what it will add.
:::

For the compute-heavy steps whose tools have **no native GPU path**: PCA,
neighbor-graph construction, UMAP, and Leiden clustering, the
[`openpipeline_rapids`](../concepts/ecosystem.qmd) package will provide
GPU-accelerated drop-in replacements via
[rapids-singlecell](https://rapids-singlecell.readthedocs.io/). It pins the core
`openpipeline` as a Viash dependency, so the pattern will be the same standard
[processing](process-samples.qmd) and [integration](integrate.qmd) flow with the
accelerated components scheduled onto a GPU.

GPU acceleration pays off on large cohorts (hundreds of thousands of cells and
up), where these steps dominate wall-clock time. For small datasets the CPU
workflows are simpler and fast enough.

:::{.callout-note}
Until the package is released, the [reference](../reference/index.qmd) shows no
`openpipeline_rapids` entries. Track progress on the package repo via Viash Hub.
:::
